EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon trough that extends all the way from the Solomon Islands southeastward to just south of the Samoa Group spread heavy rain and thunderstorms across the islands over the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain from an active monsoonal trough that had been nearly stationary just to the south of the islands caused widespread flooding across Tutuila.  Flash Flooding was reported from the Malaeimi Valley to the Bay Area.  Heavy showers and runoffs overflowed most streams from Faganeanea to Pago Pago and dumped lots of debris and rocks on the roads.  The the X-Ray room and part of the Surgical Ward at the LBJ Medical Center was flooded as the jammed and clogged up Fagaalu stream force all the water and mud onto the hospital parking lot into part of the hospital.  Forunately, the hospital had taken all necessary precautions avoiding a major disasters in protecting lives and properties.  The National Weather Service had a Flash Flood Warning, which gave enough time for the public to prepare for the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Weather Service Office recorded between 2 to 3 inches of rainfall for this event from 6 pm to 9 pm Friday evening. Residents along low-lying villages reported run-off and increasing flow of water along small streams. |Roads were covered with water; while residential yards were left with puddle of water. No injury reported.

High surf of 8 to 12 feet with occasional higher sets impacted much of south facing shores of Tutuila, Aunuu and the Manua islands in the last week of the month.  These high than normal surf were generated from a gale low far south of the islands off of New Zealand.  There were no significant damages from these surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Surf Advisories were posted for all south facing shores of Tutuila and Aunuu as heavy swell from an intense storm far south of the islands persisted for more than a week.  No significant damages were reported during this high surf episode.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another intense low pressure system far south of the islands generated heavy southerly swell across the Samoan Islands from September 14th to the 19th.  Surf of 8 to 14 feet impacted most of south faciing shores especially from Avau to Fatumafuti and from Lauli'i to Tula.  No significant damages were reported.

Surf of 8 to 14 feet impacted most of south faciing shores especially from Avau to Fatumafuti and from Lauli'i to Tula.  No significant damages were reported.

High surf continues into September as swell generated from a low far south of the islands produced surf of 8 to 12 feet with occasional higher set from September 1st to September 5th.  No significant damages were reported.

High surf was a combination of heavy southerly swell from a gale low far south of the islands and trade wind surge from a high pressure area to the imediate south of the Samoa group.  High Surf Advisories for south facing shores of all islands were issued for this episode from August 10th to August 20th. No significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Surf Advisories were posted for all south facing shores of Tutuila from Augus 24th to August 31st.  These high surf was a result of heavy swell from an intense low pressure area far south of the islands combined with swell from strong trades as a high pressure area remained nearly staionary south of the islands in the last week of august.  No significant damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northwest winds demolished several banana plantations and broke tree branches early Thursday morning.  A fast moving convection associated with a trough pass through Manu'a and Tutuila, causing agricultural damages and flying debris across the Islands.|The Islands of Manu'a had a high gust of 46 miles an hour while Tutuila Isle recorded a high gust of 42 miles an hour at Cape Matatula.  The Weather Service Office recorded a gust of 40 miles an hour for this event.|The Weather Service Office issued a Wind Advisory around 10 am through 1 pm local time.|No injury reported.

After 10:00 am Sunday 18 February 2007, 2 bodies were found and 1 lost at sea along the mouth of Aoa village harbor which lies to the north to northeast of Tutuila Island of American Samoa. The youngest of the deceased was seen alive Saturday afternoon as he was yelling for help in the ocean while swimming.  2 residents of Aoa village jumped in the water and tried to rescue this young male. A local rescue team and divers responded to this incident and conducted a search, but were unsuccessful Saturday evening.  2 bodies were found the next day while the other remains missing.|Seas were 3 to 5 feet on this day, and all weather equipments were operating normally.|This will be the first drowning incident for American Samoa in 2007.

A Flash Flood Warning for Tutuila was issued at 800 am Monday, September 3, 2007, which updated a Small Stream Flood Advisory first issued 600 am earlier that morning.  Except for debree being washed onto the roads and mud and land slides which occurred at Malota and Fagamalo, no injuries or significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers associated with an active trough over the islands dumped almost tghree inches of rain between 800 am and 1100 am Monday, September 3, 2007. No significant damages were reported other than the usual debree being washed onto the roads by heavy runoffs and the overflow of streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell along the mountains and had again flooded low lying areas and overflowed small streams in the Tualauta County and Nuuuli Uta between 400 am and 1000 am Wednesday, September 5, 2007. Some vechicles were reported stalled at the Fagaima road and in front of the Mormon Church at Otto Ville the usual ponding area during heavy rain.
